text.title: VUI Inclusivity — Core Design Rules
text.summary: Concrete design rules for building voice interfaces that work across accents, speech impediments, age groups, languages, and noisy environments.

rule.statement: Train or select ASR models on datasets that include multiple accents, dialects, age ranges (children to elderly), gender diversity, speech impediments, and real-world noise conditions.
rule.rationale: Systems trained on narrow datasets systematically fail non-native speakers, elderly users, and people with stutters or lisps — creating an exclusionary interface.

rule.statement: Support regionally distinct language variants (e.g., US English, UK English, Australian English, Indian English) with local vocabulary and pronunciation models.
rule.rationale: Vocabulary and pronunciation differences between variants cause high error rates when only a single locale model is used.

rule.statement: Provide an accent calibration flow at onboarding: have the user read sample phrases, adapt to their speech pattern, and allow manual sensitivity adjustment.
rule.rationale: Individual calibration reduces recognition errors significantly for users whose accent is underrepresented in general models.

rule.statement: Allow users to teach the system their pronunciation for misrecognized words ("I meant [correct word]") and learn from those corrections permanently.
rule.rationale: Persistent learning closes the gap for edge-case vocabularies that cannot be addressed in general training.

rule.statement: Never interrupt or timeout mid-utterance for users who pause, use filler words ("um", "uh"), repeat, self-correct, or speak slowly.
rule.rationale: Premature timeouts exclude users with stutters, hesitant speakers, and non-native speakers who construct sentences while speaking.

rule.statement: Make silence thresholds configurable; default to a longer dwell than typical ASR defaults.
rule.rationale: Standard ASR silence thresholds are tuned for fluent native speakers and cut off slower speakers.

rule.statement: Show what the system heard (live transcription) and what it understood (intent) as separate visual elements so users can verify and correct.
rule.rationale: Separating heard vs understood lets users spot recognition errors before they cause unwanted actions.

rule.statement: Never build a voice-only path. Every VUI feature must be reachable via touch or keyboard, and every voice response must have a visual equivalent.
rule.rationale: WCAG requires perceivable and operable interfaces via multiple modalities; voice-only interfaces exclude deaf users and fail in loud environments.

rule.statement: Always display available commands or a "help" prompt so users do not need to memorize the grammar.
rule.rationale: Discoverability is the primary learnability problem in VUI; users abandon systems they cannot explore.

rule.statement: Provide multi-channel confirmation for critical actions: voice response, visible status, haptic feedback, and/or sound cue — all conveying the same information.
rule.rationale: Redundant channels ensure confirmation reaches users regardless of context (noisy room, visual impairment, hands occupied).

rule.statement: Make the mute button permanently visible and provide a "stop listening" voice command. Allow users to delete recordings and disable always-on listening per feature.
rule.rationale: Privacy concerns are the top barrier to VUI adoption; transparent control builds the trust required for continued use.

rule.statement: State clearly what voice data is stored, for how long, and who has access — before the user enables voice features.
rule.rationale: Opaque data practices cause abandonment and regulatory exposure under GDPR and similar laws.

rule.statement: Include diverse testers in usability sessions: varied accents, non-native speakers, people with speech impediments, children, elderly users, and quiet vs noisy environments.
rule.rationale: Recognition accuracy metrics from homogeneous lab conditions do not predict real-world failure rates for underrepresented groups.
